title: Extent of extensive agriculture
subtitle: The extent of extensive agriculture is defined as ‘minimal’ (less than 1% of land area); ‘common’ (1 to 20% of land area) or ‘widespread’ (more than 20%).
caption:
Extent of extensive agriculture

The extent of extensive agriculture is defined as ‘minimal’ (less than 1% of land area); ‘common’ (1 to 20% of land area) or ‘widespread’ (more than 20%).

This choropleth world map displays the extent of extensive agriculture in 1850, categorized by the percentage of land area utilized. The legend distinguishes four states: minimal (light yellow), common (light green), widespread (dark green), and no data (grey or hatched). Dark green areas indicating widespread agriculture, covering more than 20% of land, are visible in specific regions including the United Kingdom, Spain, parts of Central Europe, the eastern United States, India, China, and scattered areas across Sub-Saharan Africa and Southeast Asia. Light green areas, representing common agriculture between 1% and 20%, cover large portions of North America, South America, Australia, and much of the African and Asian continents outside the widespread zones. Light yellow areas denoting minimal agriculture, less than 1%, appear in Canada, Russia, the Sahara Desert, the Arabian Peninsula, and parts of Australia. Grey shading indicating no agriculture is present in Greenland and parts of the Middle East and North Africa. A distinct hatched pattern marks South America, specifically Brazil and surrounding territories, as having no data for this metric. The visual distribution shows concentrated agricultural activity in Europe, South Asia, and East Asia, while vast northern latitudes and arid regions show minimal or no extensive agriculture.
